disclaimer: This feed reports that an official source page changed, with the passage that changed. It does not assert what the law is, and it is not legal advice. Every item was reviewed by a named human before publication. An empty `changes` array means no human has confirmed a change yet — it is NOT a claim that nothing changed at any watched source.

registry_verification.statement: THE SOURCE REGISTRY IS NOT HUMAN-VERIFIED. Each entry is a CANDIDATE official URL: our own crawler fetched it and read its title, and its `verification_status` says whether a named human has since confirmed it is the official page for that document class in that jurisdiction. `unverified` means nobody has. A machine cannot tell a state's real birth-certificate page from a convincing-looking one — it cannot even tell a live page from a bot-wall served with HTTP 200 — so do not rely on an unverified entry as authoritative guidance, and do not present it to anyone as one. What this tool does claim: this URL changed, and here is what changed in it. What it never claims: what the law is.

sources.authority: California Department of Public Health, Vital Records
sources.verification_statement: UNVERIFIED — machine-checked, not human-confirmed. A live fetch confirmed this URL answers and its title was read; NO HUMAN has confirmed that it is the official page for this document class in this jurisdiction. Do not rely on it as authoritative guidance.
sources.notes: The issuing office for the amended certificate the court order produces. THE URL IS CORRECT AND THE PAGE IS LIVE, but www.cdph.ca.gov SERVES A BROKEN CERTIFICATE CHAIN: its leaf is issued by 'Sectigo Public Server Authentication CA OV R36' while the chain it presents contains a different, stale intermediate ('Sectigo RSA Organization Validation Secure Server CA'). Browsers and curl paper over this by chasing the AIA extension to fetch the missing intermediate; Python/OpenSSL does not, so our fetcher gets CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED. Proven on 2026-07-13: supplying the AIA intermediate by hand makes the same request return 200. WE DO NOT DISABLE TLS VERIFICATION TO WORK AROUND THIS — a watcher serving people in hostile jurisdictions does not accept unverifiable TLS, and a source we cannot fetch safely is one we report as unreachable. The fix belongs to CDPH's server operators.

sources.authority: Hawaii State Legislature — HRS §286-102 (licensing is administered by the four COUNTIES; Hawaii has no state DMV)
sources.verification_statement: UNVERIFIED — machine-checked, not human-confirmed. A live fetch confirmed this URL answers and its title was read; NO HUMAN has confirmed that it is the official page for this document class in this jurisdiction. Do not rely on it as authoritative guidance.
sources.notes: live-fetched. HTTP 200, heading '§286-102 Licensing.' PARTIAL GAP FIX, and the partiality is the point: Hawaii has NO state DMV — driver licensing is administered by the four counties (Honolulu, Hawai'i, Maui, Kaua'i), so there is no statewide issuing authority whose page we could watch. What IS statewide is the statute the counties administer, HRS §286-102, and that is what this entry watches. It does NOT claim to watch a county licensing process. The county surfaces were tried: honolulu.gov/csd answers but titles its driver-licensing page with a rotating NEWS HEADLINE (false-drift on a slow cycle); hawaiicounty.gov 403s our UA; the Maui and Kaua'i pages 404. MACHINE FACT: capitol.hawaii.gov 403s under concurrent load and answers a single request fine — at a weekly cadence of one fetch this is not expected to bite, but if this source starts flapping, suspect rate-limiting before suspecting removal.

sources.notes: NOT REACHABLE, BUT NOT A 404. www.ilsos.gov fails at the connection layer, not the HTTP layer: HTTP/2 requests die with INTERNAL_ERROR and HTTP/1.1 requests time out with zero bytes received — including against the bare host root, and from two independent network vantage points. There is no status code to read, so there is nothing to call dead. Left in place with the machine facts recorded rather than deleted on a guess: a host that will not complete a TLS/HTTP exchange is an outage or a network-level block, and neither is a policy change. Still a landing page rather than a deep link; a verifier with a working route to ilsos.gov should replace it with the specific change-of-information page.

The PDF rendering of the same section carries no ticker, and carries no embedded /CreationDate that would re-stamp the bytes on every build. HONEST LIMIT: a PDF is hashed as raw bytes, so drift here says 'the bytes changed, go read it' and produces no passage diff. This is a statutory surface, not MDHHS's process page — the entry claims only that this is an official Michigan page for this document class.

sources.authority: Office of the Federal Register (National Archives)
sources.verification_statement: UNVERIFIED — machine-checked, not human-confirmed. A live fetch confirmed this URL answers and its title was read; NO HUMAN has confirmed that it is the official page for this document class in this jurisdiction. Do not rely on it as authoritative guidance.
sources.notes: REPAIRED 2026-07-13. The previous URL was the Federal Register's HTML search page (/documents/search?...), which federalregister.gov's robots.txt explicitly Disallows. Our fetcher honours robots.txt without appeal, so that entry was not merely noisy — it was UNFETCHABLE BY CONSTRUCTION and would have failed silently forever. Replaced with the public JSON API, which robots.txt does not disallow and which returns 200. This is the swap docs/ROADMAP.md M3 anticipated. LIMIT, stated plainly: the API returns single-line JSON, so it normalizes to one passage and a diff shows the whole line changing rather than a tidy per-rule delta. It still names the new rule titles in the diff, which is the signal that matters, and it is strictly better than a source we could never legally fetch.
